title: How Yasujir≈ç Ozu Learned to Use Color in His Masterful Films: A New Every Frame a Painting Video Essay
description: Yasu¬≠jir≈ç Ozu was born in 1903, and made films from the late nine¬≠teen-twen¬≠ties up until his death in 1963. Though not an espe¬≠cial¬≠ly long
body:
Yasu¬≠jir≈ç Ozu was born in 1903, and made films from the late nine¬≠teen-twen¬≠ties up until his death in 1963. Though not an espe¬≠cial¬≠ly long life, it spanned Japan‚Äôs pre- and post¬≠war eras, mean¬≠ing that in many ways, it end¬≠ed in a very dif¬≠fer¬≠ent coun¬≠try than it began. Not that you‚Äôd know it from Ozu‚Äôs films, whose dis¬≠tinc¬≠tive form and style must have changed less through the decades than those of any of his col¬≠leagues. For view¬≠ers only casu¬≠al¬≠ly acquaint¬≠ed with his oeu¬≠vre, it‚Äôs easy to joke that if you‚Äôve seen one of his pic¬≠tures, you‚Äôve seen them all. But true Ozu enthu¬≠si¬≠asts, whose num¬≠bers have steadi¬≠ly grown all around the world since the film¬≠mak¬≠er‚Äôs death, under¬≠stand that each phase of his career offers dis¬≠tinc¬≠tive plea¬≠sures of its own.
In fact, Ozu per¬≠sist¬≠ed through sweep¬≠ing changes in not just world his¬≠to¬≠ry, but also the his¬≠to¬≠ry of cin¬≠e¬≠ma. His first 34 films were silent, the next four¬≠teen were sound in black-and-white, and his last six were in col¬≠or. It is to the domes¬≠tic mas¬≠ter‚Äôs third act that Tony Zhou and Tay¬≠lor Ramos have devot¬≠ed their lat¬≠est¬ÝEvery Frame a Paint¬≠ing video essay.
As with most film¬≠mak¬≠ers, it took Ozu a few years to make col¬≠or his own: in Equinox Flower, from 1958, ‚Äúsome of the scenes are so bright that it looks like an MGM musi¬≠cal,‚Äù owing to his stu¬≠dio‚Äôs desire to show¬≠case the actress Fujiko Yamamo¬≠to. And it‚Äôs not just the hues of her kimono that dom¬≠i¬≠nate the images: so does the red of Ozu‚Äôs sig¬≠na¬≠ture teapot when¬≠ev¬≠er it finds its way into the frame.
Ozu‚Äôs next col¬≠or film¬ÝGood Morn¬≠ing makes use of a ‚Äúmuch more nat¬≠ur¬≠al, earth-toned col¬≠or palette. The images feel more bal¬≠anced, and there isn‚Äôt one visu¬≠al ele¬≠ment that sticks out from all the oth¬≠ers.‚Äù In his project after that, Float¬≠ing Weeds (itself a remake of his 1934 silent A Sto¬≠ry of Float¬≠ing Weeds), he worked with the acclaimed cin¬≠e¬≠matog¬≠ra¬≠ph¬≠er Kazuo Miya¬≠gawa, who‚Äôd also col¬≠lab¬≠o¬≠rat¬≠ed with the likes of Kuro¬≠sawa and Mizoguchi. Using strong light and shad¬≠ow, Miya¬≠gawa showed how, ‚Äúby shap¬≠ing the light, he could change how col¬≠ors were per¬≠ceived,‚Äù often in dif¬≠fer¬≠ent scenes framed in exact¬≠ly the same way. At this point, any¬≠one doing an Ozu binge-watch will feel that col¬≠or itself is being adapt¬≠ed to the rig¬≠or¬≠ous objec¬≠tiv¬≠i¬≠ty of his work.


	

‚ÄúHis films are full of rep¬≠e¬≠ti¬≠tions and small vari¬≠a¬≠tions,‚Äù Zhou says. ‚ÄúHe will show the same hall¬≠way again, and again, and again.‚Äù Seem¬≠ing¬≠ly minor ele¬≠ments in one scene match visu¬≠al¬≠ly with ele¬≠ments in oth¬≠ers. ‚ÄúAs a result, Ozu‚Äôs movies rhyme. One shot will mir¬≠ror anoth¬≠er, one per¬≠son¬≠‚Äôs behav¬≠ior will be repeat¬≠ed,‚Äù across not just an indi¬≠vid¬≠ual pic¬≠ture, but his whole fil¬≠mog¬≠ra¬≠phy. Watch through it, and¬Ý‚Äúyou‚Äôre struck by how sim¬≠i¬≠lar two peo¬≠ple can be, how often one place resem¬≠bles anoth¬≠er, how life itself is cycli¬≠cal, and Ozu used col¬≠or as anoth¬≠er way to build these pat¬≠terns.‚Äù Though sub¬≠tly expressed, these themes would cer¬≠tain¬≠ly have res¬≠onat¬≠ed with audi¬≠ences in a soci¬≠ety forced to rein¬≠vent itself after los¬≠ing the Sec¬≠ond World War. Whether Ozu sus¬≠pect¬≠ed that they could draw even more atten¬≠tion from future gen¬≠er¬≠a¬≠tions far from Japan is a ques¬≠tion¬Ýnot even his diaries, now the sub¬≠ject of a doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary them¬≠selves, can answer.
Relat¬≠ed con¬≠tent:
An Intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to Yasu¬≠jir≈ç Ozu, ‚Äúthe Most Japan¬≠ese of All Film Direc¬≠tors‚Äù
How One Sim¬≠ple Cut Reveals the Cin¬≠e¬≠mat¬≠ic Genius of Yasu¬≠jir≈ç Ozu
The Gold¬≠en Age of Japan¬≠ese Cin¬≠e¬≠ma: Kuro¬≠sawa, Ozu, Mizoguchi & Beyond
Wes Ander¬≠son & Yasu¬≠jiro Ozu: New Video Essay Reveals the Unex¬≠pect¬≠ed Par¬≠al¬≠lels Between Two Great Film¬≠mak¬≠ers
How Mas¬≠ter Japan¬≠ese Ani¬≠ma¬≠tor Satoshi Kon Pushed the Bound¬≠aries of Mak¬≠ing Ani¬≠me: A Video Essay
Every Frame a Paint¬≠ing Returns to YouTube & Explores Why the Sus¬≠tained Two-Shot Van¬≠ished from Movies
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Buckminster Fuller Creates an Animated Visualization of Human Population Growth from 1000 B.C.E. to 1965
description:
in Design, Maps |   	May 7th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Sit back, relax, put on some music (I‚Äôve found Chopin‚Äôs Noc¬≠turne in B major well-suit¬≠ed),
body:
in Design, Maps |   	May 7th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Sit back, relax, put on some music (I‚Äôve found Chopin‚Äôs Noc¬≠turne in B major well-suit¬≠ed), and watch the video above, a silent data visu¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion by vision¬≠ary archi¬≠tect and sys¬≠tems the¬≠o¬≠rist Buck¬≠min¬≠ster Fuller, ‚Äúthe James Brown of indus¬≠tri¬≠al design.‚Äù The short film from 1965 com¬≠bines two of Fuller‚Äôs lead¬≠ing con¬≠cerns: the expo¬≠nen¬≠tial spread¬Ýof the human pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion over finite mass¬≠es of land and the need to revise our glob¬≠al per¬≠spec¬≠tive via the¬Ý‚ÄúDymax¬≠ion map,‚Äù in order ‚Äúto visu¬≠al¬≠ize the whole plan¬≠et with greater accu¬≠ra¬≠cy,‚Äù as the Buck¬≠min¬≠ster Fuller Insti¬≠tute writes, so that ‚Äúwe humans will be bet¬≠ter equipped to address chal¬≠lenges as we face our com¬≠mon future aboard Space¬≠ship Earth.‚Äù
Though you may know it best as the name of a geo¬≠des¬≠ic sphere at Disney‚Äôs Epcot Cen¬≠ter, the term Space¬≠ship Earth orig¬≠i¬≠nal¬≠ly came from Fuller, who used it to remind us of our inter¬≠con¬≠nect¬≠ed¬≠ness and inter¬≠de¬≠pen¬≠dence as we share resources on the only vehi¬≠cle we know of that can sus¬≠tain us in the cos¬≠mos.
‚ÄúWe are all astro¬≠nauts,‚Äù he wrote in his 1969 Oper¬≠at¬≠ing Man¬≠u¬≠al for Space¬≠ship Earth, and yet we refuse to see the long-term con¬≠se¬≠quences of our actions on our spe¬≠cial¬≠ized craft: ‚ÄúOne of the rea¬≠sons why we are strug¬≠gling inad¬≠e¬≠quate¬≠ly today,‚Äù Fuller argued in his intro¬≠duc¬≠tion, ‚Äúis that we reck¬≠on our costs on too short¬≠sight¬≠ed a basis and are lat¬≠er over¬≠whelmed with the unex¬≠pect¬≠ed costs brought about by our short¬≠sight¬≠ed¬≠ness.‚Äù

Like all vision¬≠ar¬≠ies, Fuller thought in long spans of time, and he used his design skills to help oth¬≠ers¬Ýdo so as well. His pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion visu¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion¬Ýdoc¬≠u¬≠ments human¬Ýgrowth from 1000 B.C.E. to Fuller‚Äôs present, at the time, of 1965. In the image above (see a larg¬≠er ver¬≠sion here), we have a graph¬≠ic from that same year‚Äîmade col¬≠lab¬≠o¬≠ra¬≠tive¬≠ly with artist and soci¬≠ol¬≠o¬≠gist John McHale‚Äîshowing the ‚Äúshrink¬≠ing of our plan¬≠et by man‚Äôs increased trav¬≠el and com¬≠mu¬≠ni¬≠ca¬≠tion speeds around the globe.‚Äù (It must be near micro¬≠scop¬≠ic by now.) Fuller takes an even longer view, look¬≠ing at ‚Äúthe con¬≠flu¬≠ence of com¬≠mu¬≠ni¬≠ca¬≠tion and trans¬≠porta¬≠tion tech¬≠nolo¬≠gies,‚Äù writes Rikke Schmidt Kj√¶r¬≠gaard, ‚Äúfrom 500,000 B.C.E. to 1965.‚Äù
Here Fuller com¬≠bines his pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion data with the tech¬≠no¬≠log¬≠i¬≠cal break¬≠throughs of moder¬≠ni¬≠ty. Though he‚Äôs thought of in some quar¬≠ters as a genius and in some as a kook, Fuller demon¬≠strat¬≠ed his tremen¬≠dous fore¬≠sight in seem¬≠ing¬≠ly innu¬≠mer¬≠able ways. But it was in the realm of design that he excelled in com¬≠mu¬≠ni¬≠cat¬≠ing what he saw. ‚ÄúPio¬≠neers of data visu¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion,‚Äù Fuller and McHale were two of ‚Äúthe first to chart long-term trends of indus¬≠tri¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion and glob¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion.‚Äù Instead of becom¬≠ing alarmed and fear¬≠ful of what the trends showed, Fuller got to work design¬≠ing for the future, ful¬≠ly aware, writes the Fuller Insti¬≠tute, that ‚Äúthe plan¬≠et is a sys¬≠tem, and a resilient one.‚Äù
Fuller thought like a rad¬≠i¬≠cal¬≠ly inven¬≠tive engi¬≠neer, but he spoke and wrote like a peacenik prophet, writ¬≠ing that a sys¬≠tem of nar¬≠row spe¬≠cial¬≠iza¬≠tions ensures that skill sets ‚Äúare not com¬≠pre¬≠hend¬≠ed com¬≠pre¬≠hen¬≠sive¬≠ly‚Ä¶ or they are real¬≠ized only in neg¬≠a¬≠tive ways, in new weapon¬≠ry or the indus¬≠tri¬≠al sup¬≠port only of war far¬≠ing.‚Äù We‚Äôve seen this vision of soci¬≠ety played out to a fright¬≠en¬≠ing extent. Fuller saw a way out, one in which every¬≠one on the plan¬≠et can live in com¬≠fort and secu¬≠ri¬≠ty with¬≠out con¬≠sum¬≠ing (then not renew¬≠ing) the Earth‚Äôs resources. How can this be done? You‚Äôll have to read Fuller‚Äôs work to find out. Mean¬≠while, as his visu¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tions sug¬≠gest, it‚Äôs best for us to take the long view‚Äîand give up on short-term rewards and profits‚Äîin our assess¬≠ments of the state of Space¬≠ship Earth.
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
Buck¬≠min¬≠ster Fuller‚Äôs Map of the World: The Inno¬≠va¬≠tion that Rev¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion¬≠ized Map Design (1943)
How the Human Pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion Reached 8 Bil¬≠lion: An Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed Video Cov¬≠ers 300,000 Years of His¬≠to¬≠ry in Four Min¬≠utes
The Life & Times of Buck¬≠min¬≠ster Fuller‚Äôs Geo¬≠des¬≠ic Dome: A Doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary
A Visu¬≠al¬≠iza¬≠tion of the Unit¬≠ed States‚Äô Explod¬≠ing Pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion Growth Over 200 Years (1790 ‚Äì 2010)
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.

title: Luscious Hair Sculptures Sprout Like Branches in a Symbiotic Exhibition
description: A visit to Lincoln Park or the Garfield Park Conservatory is one of the outings Chicagoans rarely pass up, particularly when we need some reprieve
body:
A visit to Lincoln Park or the Garfield Park Conservatory is one of the outings Chicagoans rarely pass up, particularly when we need some reprieve from all the concrete and steel. Two beloved green spaces in the city, these spots boast oases blanketed in verdant foliage even in the depths of winter and house an array of specimens not native to the Midwest. 
For artists Merryn Omotayo Alaka and Sam Fr√©squez, the immersive nature of a conservancy, with plants above and below and all around, became a central point for a collaborative project. Your Birth is My Birth presents the duo‚Äôs synthetic hair sculptures, which suspend from the ceiling of Jane Lombard Gallery and splay across the wooden floor like organic growths. Alaka and Fr√©squez describe the exhibition as a sort of ‚ÄúKanekalon forest,‚Äù referring to the brand behind the luscious material.
Installation view of ‚ÄòYour Birth is My Birth.‚Äô Photo by Adam Reich
Five different ‚Äúspecies‚Äù emerge in the space, including Listening Roots,¬ÝHearing Bells,¬ÝMother & Child,¬ÝStacking Pearls, and¬ÝUmbra Pods. Dark, dyed locks and domed shapes are throughlines, although each takes on a distinctive form. The series are influenced by epiphytes, non-parasitic plants that make their homes on a host specimen. Think orchids, cacti, moss, and kelp.
Surging upward from lily pad-shaped discs suctioned to the floorboards, the largest sculpture is part of Listening Roots, which tethers singular shoots to a central form. This connection between smaller pieces‚Äîlike the feather-duster-shaped Stacking Pearls‚Äîand more comprehensive structures recurs throughout the exhibition, gesturing toward an intimate and intentional symbiosis.
Several works also reference genetics and what‚Äôs passed down through generations, as mirrored forms emerge within the same vertical tendril. ‚ÄúSimilar to an epiphyte and its host tree, these sculptural works have their own life cycles evoking systems of dependence and exchange, where one form sustains from another,‚Äù says a statement.
Your Birth is My Birth is on view through June 13. Explore more from Alaka and Fr√©squez on Instagram.
‚ÄúStacking Pearl (Adolescent) I‚Äù (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 24 x 24 inches
Installation view of ‚ÄòYour Birth is My Birth.‚Äô Photo by Adam Reich
Detail of ‚ÄúUmbra Pods I‚Äù (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 45 x 27 inches
Installation view of ‚ÄòYour Birth is My Birth.‚Äô Photo by Adam Reich
Detail of ‚ÄúUmbra Pods III‚Äù (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 45 x 27 inches
Installation view of ‚ÄòYour Birth is My Birth.‚Äô Photo by Adam Reich

title: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ‚ÄòVisual Activism‚Äô in Her Richly Layered Illustrations
description: ‚ÄúTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations t
body:
‚ÄúTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations that need to be addressed,‚Äù says Fatinha Ramos. ‚ÄúIt is the only way I can truly connect with others.‚Äù
Based in Antwerp, the Portuguese artist and illustrator is well-known for blending analog and digital techniques to create rich, emotive compositions. Collaborating with clients like The New York Times, The Washington Post, Tate, and Scientific American, among many others, Ramos has cultivated a keen eye for storytelling through her distinctive visual language.

Recent partnerships include the Anne Frank Museum and MoMA, the latter of which commissioned the artist to illustrate an essay on her experience being compared to Frida Kahlo. Ramos was born with osteogenesis imperfecta, commonly called brittle bone disease, which spurred a childhood spent in and out of hospitals. Drawing and art-making quickly became a preferred pastime, allowing her to transport herself from such clinical settings.
This adolescent hobby stuck, and Ramos worked as an art director in advertising and publishing for 12 years before venturing out on her own. Boasting an impressive list of clients and collaborators, she considers her practice to be an antidote to stereotypical narratives, whether related to the climate crisis, sexism, racism, or the dire lack of empathy that seems rampant in today‚Äôs world.
‚ÄúI want to move away from the narrative that turns artists with disabilities into symbols of resilience,‚Äù she tells MoMA. ‚ÄúCreativity does not happen despite limitations but through them. Art should broaden how we see the world‚Äîand that includes how we see bodies, too.‚Äù
At the moment, Ramos is only accepting select illustration clients as she focuses on her fine art practice, including a series of anatomical glass sculptures based on brittle bone disease. You can explore more of her practice on her website and Instagram.

schema.headline: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ‚ÄòVisual Activism‚Äô in Her Richly Layered Illustrations

title: Explore 25 Incredible Photos of the Milky Way Captured Around the World
description: Organized by Capture the Atlas, the 2026 Milky Way Photographer of the Year saw a record number of submissions, with more than 6,500 entries repre
body:
Organized by Capture the Atlas, the 2026 Milky Way Photographer of the Year saw a record number of submissions, with more than 6,500 entries representing a wide range of landscapes and perspectives around the world. Just 25 were selected as the top images, representing 12 different regions from the Canary Islands to New Zealand to Argentina.
‚ÄúEvery year, this collection reminds us that photographing the Milky Way is not only about technique or planning. It is about curiosity, patience, and the desire to experience the night sky in places where it still feels wild,‚Äù says Dan Zafra, editor of Capture the Atlas and curator of the annual contest. ‚ÄúMany of these skies are becoming increasingly rare, and we hope these images inspire people not only to admire them, but also to value and protect them.‚Äù
Brendan Larsen, ‚ÄúMilky Way over Syme Hut and Mt. Taranaki.‚Äù Syme Hut, Mt Taranaki, New Zealand‚Äã
Max Terwindt, ‚ÄúCaldera Galaxy Panorama.‚Äù La Palma, Canary Islands
Lukasz Remkowicz, ‚ÄúMilky Way over the Tatra Mountains.‚Äù Jurg√≥w, Poland
Leonel Padron, ‚ÄúLost in the Ripples of Space and Time.‚Äù Pinnacles Desert, Nambung, Western Australia
Anastasia Gulova, ‚ÄúGalaxy on the Rise.‚Äù Tenerife, Canary Islands, Spain
Daniel Vi√±√© Garcia, ‚ÄúMy Perfect Night.‚Äù Catamarca, Argentina
Anthony Lopez, ‚ÄúCelestial Light over Sea Cliffs.‚Äù Saint Rapha√´l, French Riviera
Uro≈° Fink, ‚ÄúGeminid Symphony Over La Palma‚Äôs Guardian of the Sky.‚Äù Roque de los Muchachos GTC Telescope, La Palma‚Äã

title: Jake Messing's Hyperrealistic Paintings Celebrate the Abundance of Nature
description: ‚ÄúThe world hums with beauty and danger, harmony and discord,‚Äù says Jake Messing. ‚ÄúWe walk through these shifting currents every day. For as long a
body:
‚ÄúThe world hums with beauty and danger, harmony and discord,‚Äù says Jake Messing. ‚ÄúWe walk through these shifting currents every day. For as long as I can remember, I have turned toward the natural world‚Äîstudying its patterns, its relationships, its quiet lessons.‚Äù
In highly detailed, hyperrealistic paintings, the Northern California-based artist explores nature as a reflection of our inner lives. Abundance and beauty are sometimes confronted with tension and discomfort, and through nature, ‚ÄúI question the fears and unspoken rules that shape us,‚Äù Messing says.
‚ÄúCoccinellidaes Hideaway 2‚Äù
Working in acrylic on canvas, the artist composes otherworldly vignettes of flora and fauna, often uniting creatures and plants in situations we‚Äôd be unlikely to encounter in the real world. Yet these dense, maximal clusters of succulents, insects, blossoms, birds, and other creatures summon what Messing describes as both ‚Äúchaos and grace‚Äù in a vibrant meditation on ecosystems,  interdependency, and biodiversity.
In an art historical sense, these works certainly nod to the meticulously detailed Dutch Golden Age oil paintings of the likes of Rachel Ruysch and Jan Brueghel the Elder, which were also typically set against deep backgrounds. Employing a bit of memento mori‚Äîa reminder of the inevitability of death‚Äîthese often incorporated wilting petals and other nods to decay.
Messing taps into this tradition, yet he emphasizes full-blooded vivacity. Every floret and frond is bursting with life, while the occasional playful color gradient, bubbles, or shiny fabric place these compositions firmly in our time. ‚ÄúThrough my work, I seek to bring the outside in, to honor the wildness that surrounds us, and to reveal the beauty and danger, the decay and renewal, that bind our outer and inner worlds together,‚Äù he says.
See more on the artist‚Äôs Instagram.
‚ÄúBubbles and Blooms‚Äù
Detail of ‚ÄúBubbles and Blooms‚Äù
‚ÄúVisible Light‚Äù
‚ÄúSwarms and Swallows‚Äù
‚ÄúAzure Guard‚Äù
Detail of ‚ÄúAzure Guard‚Äù
‚ÄúBeasts and Beauty‚Äù
‚ÄúSequined Spring‚Äù
‚ÄúFoiled Florals‚Äù

title: NASA Just Dropped More Than 12,000 Photos from the Artemis II Mission
description: When it comes to photo dumps, NASA has upped the ante. The organization has added thousands of snapshots from the Artemis II mission to the Gatewa
body:
When it comes to photo dumps, NASA has upped the ante. The organization has added thousands of snapshots from the Artemis II mission to the Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth archive. The album now holds 12,217 images by cosmic travelers Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen during their more than 250,000-mile, 10-day flyby mission around the moon.
According to PetaPixel, a couple of Nikons and an iPhone 17 were the cameras of choice for the journey. And even though many of the thousands of recently uploaded images are very similar‚Äîsome are even quite blurry‚Äîscrolling through them gives the impression of being seated right next to the ‚ÄúMoonfarers‚Äù as they marvel at Earth and its satellite and simply can‚Äôt put the camera down‚Äîjust like we tend to snap way too many photos of a beautiful sunset.

Some of the most impactful photos include the reflections of the astronauts‚Äô hands and faces in the window of their vehicle, the Orion module. Juxtaposed with meticulously engineered equipment, the earth and moon seem somehow less abstracted from this unique vantage point, in which these orbs appear somehow more resonant and precious‚Äîand vulnerable.
Explore more of our favorites below, and learn about the Artemis II mission on NASA‚Äôs site.

schema.headline: NASA Just Dropped More Than 12,000 Photos from the Artemis II Mission
